title: Salmon Rice Bowl
tagline: The viral salmon bowl, seared fresh instead of microwaved.
description: Crispy-skinned seared salmon over warm rice with kimchi, avocado, and the 'everything' sauce (Kewpie mayo, soy, sriracha). Inspired by Emily Mariko's viral leftover-salmon bowl - hers flakes microwaved leftovers with an ice cube; this is the from-scratch version with skin worth eating.
ingredients: Diamond Crystal kosher salt and black pepper
steps: Mix all sauce ingredients in a small bowl. Set aside.
Pat salmon dry with paper towels. Season skin side with salt. Season flesh side with salt and pepper.
Heat oil in a non-stick skillet over medium-high heat. Place salmon skin-side down. Press gently with a spatula for the first 30 seconds to prevent curling.
Cook 4-5 minutes until skin is deeply crispy and releases easily. Flip and cook 2-3 more minutes - pull at 125°F internal for medium (or 145°F if you want USDA-done).
Divide rice between two bowls. Top with kimchi, avocado slices, and nori strips.
Place salmon on top, skin-side up so it stays crunchy. Drizzle generously with sauce.
Garnish with green onions and sesame seeds. Serve immediately.
notes: The crispy skin is the point. Bone-dry salmon, hot pan, don't rush the skin side.
Kewpie mayo is richer than regular mayo (extra yolk, MSG) - Asian grocery or the international aisle.
Add a jammy egg (6½-min boil) for extra protein.

The original: Mariko flakes leftover salmon into rice, microwaves it with an ice cube on top for steam, then adds the same sauce. Works great with leftovers - this recipe is for when you're starting fresh.
testedNote: Dropped the false 'crispy rice' claim (nothing crisped the rice), added a salmon pull temp, and credited Mariko's actual method.

verdict.note: The sauce carries it - make double and put it on everything.

troubleshooting: Skin stuck to the pan → fish was wet or pan wasn't hot; dry hard, heat until the oil shimmers, and don't move it early.
Skin crisp then turned soggy → sat skin-side down in the bowl; always plate skin-up.
Sauce tastes flat → regular mayo missing the Kewpie richness; add a pinch of sugar and a drop of sesame oil.
